evidence_quotes.quote: If a publisher ends their support for games that require or are set up to use an internet connection the game may become completely unplayable despite being an outright purchase.
There should be an 'end of service plan' required for all games to ensure that buyers can play their games for as long as they want.

There might be a point in the purchase agreement that mentions the possibility of the game being arbitrarily shut down or otherwise disabled by the publisher, this is most often hidden deep within the agreement and written as legalese.

Payment processors (presumably MasterCard and Visa, but possibly others) have inserted themselves into the domain of lawmakers! They put pressure on "Steam" and "itch.io" (two gaming platforms on the internet) to stop the sale and/or hide games that while "legal" MAY violate the standards of the payment processors.

Since prohibiting "AI" entirely is unrealistic I would like to suggest that a label should be created by the EU that certify digital products, that do not use "AI" (including "procedurally generated" content) to give EU consumers the option to chose "AI"-free products easier.

paragraphs.text: There are three injustices I would like to have addressed! Currently there is no clear legislation on games that require online functionality (e.g. PC games). If a publisher ends their support for games that do require or are set up to use an internet connection the game may become completely unplayable despite being an outright purchase. While there might be a point in the purchase agreement that mentions the possibility of the game being arbitrarily shut down or otherwise disabled by the publisher, this is most often hidden deep within the agreement and written as legalese. Ordinary people will not read license agreements or purchase agreements in full, especially if they are 30+ pages long legal text that is sometimes hard to interpret. Clearly this is a situation in which the balance of power is 100% in the favor of the gaming industry and disadvantages all consumers. Furthermore games can and should be considered art. Once disabled as described above that art is gone, possibly for forever. It is important not to allow art to be destroyed in this manner or it will eventually spread to other areas for the sake of profit! There should be legislation to ensure that games can not just be taken by the developer and/or publisher from the buyer! There should be an "end of service plan" required for all games to ensure that buyers can play their games for as long as they want. The excuse by the industry that the purchase is not of a game, but only a license agreement to play the game does not stand up to scrutiny in my opinion. The license agreement does not contain an expiration date for one and even if it did, this would be a bad view to take on video games, because they are or should be considered art and making the games unplayable is effectively destroying art. We, humans as a whole, have already decided that destroying art for any reason is bad, much less to force people to buy new games to replace the old ones... Recently another injustice has happened! Payment processors (presumably MasterCard and Visa, but possibly others) have inserted themselves into the domain of lawmakers! They put pressure on "Steam" and "itch.io" (two gaming platforms on the internet) to stop the sale and/or hide games that while "legal" MAY violate the standards of the payment processors. This is a massive overreach of these unspecified payment processors! I am not versed enough in EU law to be certain that an equivalent exists, but in the USA there is a law on tortuous interference. In short: If someone interjects him-/herself into a legal business transaction and interferes such that it prevents the transaction can take place this would be considered illegal. There are similar concepts in EU law I believe? This situation was instigated primarily by a group from Australia (Collective Shout) who were maybe to some degree correct to appose an illegal game (illegal in Australia) that had made it into the Australian market on Steam (The game: "No mercy"). They did however go to far by demanding, that even legal games be removed from "Steam" by calling on the payment processors. They later also threatened "itch.io" that they would do the same to that much smaller platform which prompted "itch.io" to make searching for many of the legal games on their platform impossible. IF payment processors are allowed to interfere in legal transactions in this manner there is a very real thread that groups like "Collective Shout" could impose their view on the entire world again and make even ordinarily uncontroversial games disappear from platforms despite them being legal in most if not all EU countries. A Third Problem is so call "AI" which will be a much bigger Problem in the future! Since prohibiting "AI" entirely is unrealistic I would like to suggest that a label should be created by the EU that certify digital products, that do not use "AI" (including "procedurally generated" content) to give EU consumers the option to chose "AI"-free products easier.
